coredata.dc:title: Ce-doped carbon dots enhanced by ionic liquids: A potent corrosion shield for N80 steel in aggressive 1M HCl environment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Fengting
Zhou, Yi
Wang, Zhikun
Qu, Wentao
Su, Huiling
Li, Miantuo
Cui, Nannan
Sun, Shuangqing
Fu, Yu
Hu, Songqing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been studied as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors for metals. However, the mechanisms behind their effectiveness are not fully understood. We developed a cost-effective and environmentally friendly method to synthesize Ce-doped CDs (CeCDs) using urea, ammonium citrate, and cerium nitrate. By optimizing the Ce doping concentration to 200 μg/L, we achieved a high corrosion inhibition efficiency. When tested in 1 M HCl, CeCDs at 200 mg/L showed 80 % inhibition, while ionic liquid-modified CeCDs (IL-CeCDs) demonstrated an even higher efficiency of 95.35 % on N80 steel. Both CeCDs and IL-CeCDs adsorb onto metal surfaces through a combination of physical and chemical interactions, inhibiting corrosion mainly by affecting cathodic reactions. Among different Ce-doped CDs, CeCD3N, which has a single Ce atom doping, exhibited the highest molecular reactivity, allowing it to interact more effectively with the Fe surface. Further analysis revealed that Ce doping reduces the energy gap of CDs, enhancing their reactivity. Molecular dynamics simulations showed that CeCD3N adsorbs more strongly and forms a closer inhibitor film on the Fe surface compared to other Ce-doped CDs, making it an effective corrosion inhibitor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rare-earth element
Ce
Ionic liquids
Quantum chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and particle size distributions of CeCDs (a) and IL-CeCDs (b). FTIR (c), UV absorption spectrum (d), and XPS (e∼i) spectra of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs.
Corrosion rate of N80 steel and inhibition efficiency of CeCDs (a, b) and IL-CeCDs (c) in 1 M HCl solution, as well as the adsorption isotherms (d).
Nyquist curves of CeCDs with doping concentrations of 100 (a), 200 (b), 300 (c), 400 (d), and 500 (e) μg/L, as well as Nyquist curves of IL-CeCD (f), at various dosing concentrations.
Tafel curves of CeCDs with doping concentrations of 100 (a), 200 (b), 300 (c), 400 (d), and 500 (e) μg/L, as well as Tafel curves of IL-CeCD (f), at various dosing concentrations.

SEM morphologies of steel surface after product removal (a-d). Laser confocal scanning of original N80 steel sheet (e), no corrosion inhibitor added (f), CeCDs added (g), IL-CeCDs added (h), S
z and S
a (i) values. XPS spectra of steel surface after corrosion tests in blank, CeCDs, and IL-CeCDs solutions. Full-XPS spectrum (j), and split Ce 3d (k), Fe 2p (l), and N 1 s (m) spectrum of steel surface in IL-CeCDs solution.

SEM morphologies of surface film of N80 steel in 1 M HCl after the addition of CeCDs (a-d) and IL-CeCDs (e-h) at concentrations of 50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L, as well as that of the blank sample (i). Ce element distributions of CeCDs (j) and IL-CeCDs (k). Schematic diagram of corrosion inhibitor adsorption film (l).
Frontline orbit distribution (a), differential charge density distribution (b), and electrostatic potential distribution (c) of four representative carbon dots models.

Mean square displacement (MSD) curves of four inhibitors (a), relative concentration distributions of four inhibitors and solution molecules (b), relative concentration distributions of four inhibitors (c), and averaged interaction energies between four inhibitors with iron surface (d) in the HCl solution.
Mechanism of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs corrosion inhibitors.
Synthesis process of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs.
EIS parameters of N80 steel in 1 M HCl solution with addition of different concentrations of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs.
Corrosion parameters of N80 steel in solutions containing different concentrations of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs.

head.ce:title: Ce-doped carbon dots enhanced by ionic liquids: A potent corrosion shield for N80 steel in aggressive 1M HCl environment

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Materials, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Materials, China University of Petroleum (East China)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Materials, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been studied as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors for metals. However, the mechanisms behind their effectiveness are not fully understood. We developed a cost-effective and environmentally friendly method to synthesize Ce-doped CDs (CeCDs) using urea, ammonium citrate, and cerium nitrate. By optimizing the Ce doping concentration to 200 μg/L, we achieved a high corrosion inhibition efficiency. When tested in 1 M HCl, CeCDs at 200 mg/L showed 80 % inhibition, while ionic liquid-modified CeCDs (IL-CeCDs) demonstrated an even higher efficiency of 95.35 % on N80 steel. Both CeCDs and IL-CeCDs adsorb onto metal surfaces through a combination of physical and chemical interactions, inhibiting corrosion mainly by affecting cathodic reactions. Among different Ce-doped CDs, CeCD3N, which has a single Ce atom doping, exhibited the highest molecular reactivity, allowing it to interact more effectively with the Fe surface. Further analysis revealed that Ce doping reduces the energy gap of CDs, enhancing their reactivity. Molecular dynamics simulations showed that CeCD3N adsorbs more strongly and forms a closer inhibitor film on the Fe surface compared to other Ce-doped CDs, making it an effective corrosion inhibitor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is widely used in various industries from machinery to aerospace, and it faces corrosion risks. Corrosion inhibitors that form a protective film on its surface can be used to alleviate these risks [1,2]. The toxicity of traditional corrosion inhibitors and their entry into water systems via drainage pose significant risks to the natural environment. At the same time, their long-term use harms aquatic life and disrupts ecological balance [3,4]. Heightened environmental awareness favors the use of eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors to mitigate industrial impacts and align with sustainable development goals. Cerium (Ce) corrosion inhibitor, among inorganic options, stands out for its notable advantages in low toxicity, substrate compatibility, high corrosion inhibition effect, and ease of use, thereby presenting promising applications in the realm of metal corrosion protection [5–7]. However, the use of Ce corrosion inhibitor is constrained due to its high cost, significant susceptibility to specific conditions (e.g., pH, temperature, and ion concentration), as well as poor stability.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel nanomaterial, have demonstrated extensive application prospects in the field of corrosion protection. CDs possess excellent physicochemical properties such as good water solubility, low cytotoxicity, and strong luminescent characteristics, making them outstanding in corrosion inhibition [8]. CDs form protective films on metal surfaces, effectively suppressing corrosion. For instance, functionalized CDs can enhance their corrosion resistance, maintaining protective efficacy in complex corrosion environments. Furthermore, CDs enable intelligent and precise corrosion protection through self-healing and self-warning technologies. Studies on the application of CDs on different metals have also demonstrated their broad applicability. For example, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) significantly inhibit corrosion reactions of copper in sulfuric acid solutions, with inhibition efficiencies exceeding 95 %. Additionally, biomass-based carbon dots have exhibited good corrosion inhibition performance on Q235 steel, providing new insights for the development of green and efficient corrosion inhibitors [9].

Integrating organic CDs with inorganic Ce element represents an innovative design concept promising to yield more effective corrosion inhibitors. Liu et al. [8] demonstrated this concept by synthesizing a Ce and N co-doped Ce@N-CDs corrosion inhibitor, which achieved a remarkable 96.4 % reduction of the corrosion rate of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl at the concentration of 200 mg/L. Zeng et al. [10] produced Ce and N co-doped carbon dots (Ce@N-CDs) by hydrothermal synthesis, and found that the adsorption film formed by the interaction of these CDs with unsaturated bonds on the Cu surface effectively safeguards metals from corrosion. Subash et al. [11] synthesized Cu,N-doped carbon dots (Cu,N-CDs) with different Cu doping contents as corrosion inhibitors for low carbon steel substrates. It was found that the growth of Cu deposits on the steel surface was helpful to better inhibit corrosion. However, this kind of CDs has problems such as easy aggregation and low yield, and its potential has not been fully unleashed. Ionic liquids have overcome the aforementioned difficulties and demonstrated significant advantages in the preparation and post-modification of CDs [12]. Ye et al. [13] synthesized a green and effective functional carbon dots (FCDs) corrosion inhibitor for metal protection in 1 M HCl by combining imidazole with citrate carbon dots (CA-CDs). In our previous study [14], we also synthesized CDs modified by imidazolium ionic liquid (IL-CDs) as a corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel, and found that IL reduced π-π interaction between CDs, which made IL-CDs more soluble in water and exhibited better corrosion inhibition effect than CDs.

In this work, we proposed a dual modification strategy combining inorganic Ce doping and organic ionic liquid grafting on CDs for the production of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs corrosion inhibitors. Firstly, cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3) was used to dope and modify CDs for the synthesis of CeCDs by the microwave method, and the optimal concentration of Ce(NO3)3 was explored. Then, amino-terminated imidazolium ionic liquids were synthesized and reacted with carboxyl groups at the edges of CeCDs to prepare IL-CeCDs. The molecular structures, morphologies, bonding status, and chemical composition of the obtained CeCDs and IL-CeCDs were characterized by Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Ultraviolet-visible adsorption spectroscopy (UV–vis), Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. Weight loss and electrochemical methods were used to determine the corrosion inhibition performances of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs. Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and Laser Confocal Microscope (LCM) were used to analyze the microscopic surface corrosion morphology. Furthermore, quantitative calculations and molecular dynamics simulations were used to analyze the effect of Ce doping and ionic liquid grafting on the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDs. The findings in this work are expected to provide new insights into the preparation of modified CDs as effective corrosion inhibitors in strongly acidic solution through a combined Ce doping and ionic liquid grafting strategy.

All reagents and solvents were used as purchased without further purification. Citric acid monohydrate, 1-Bromohexane, N-(3-Aminopropyl)-imidazole, Thiourea, and Triethylamine N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride were purchased from Aladdin in analytical grade. N80 steel was purchased from Shandong Yangxin County Shengxin Technology Co., Ltd.

Cerium nitrate, citric acid, and urea were dissolved in 10 mL distilled water and sonicated for 10 min. The well-mixed solution was placed in a microwave reactor at 800 W for 4 min, and then the product was dissolved in 30 mL distilled water. The solution was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min, and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (500 Da) for 12 h. The distilled water was changed every two hours. After completion of dialysis the CeCDs product was transferred to a vacuum chamber at 60℃ and dried for 48 h to obtain the product CeCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ionic liquid modified carbon dots (IL-CeCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 100 mg CeCDs and 1 g 1-ethyl-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water to form solution A. Next, 2 g imidazolium ionic liquid was dissolved in 20 mL ethanol. Then, 0.5 mL triethylamine was extracted using a syringe and injected into the ethanol solution containing the imidazolium ionic liquids, followed by thorough mixing to prepare solution B. Solution B was poured into a 250 mL round-bottom flask and heated to 60°C in an oil bath while maintaining stirring. Subsequently, solution A was slowly added dropwise to solution B. After the addition was completed, stirring was continued for 24 h to ensure complete reaction between CeCDs and imidazolium ionic liquids. Once the reaction was finished, the reaction product was transferred to a vacuum oven and dried at room temperature for 12 h. The product was then subjected to dialysis using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 Da in deionized water for 24 h. During this period, the deionized water was replaced every 2–3 h to ensure thorough dialysis. Finally, the dialyzed product was placed back in the vacuum oven and dried at 40°C for 24 h to obtain the final IL-CeCDs product. Scheme 1 illustrates the synthesis process of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs.

The corrosion inhibition performances of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs on N80 steel were determined by weight loss measurement at room temperature. All N80 steel sheets (30 mm×15 mm×3 mm) were polished using 800#, 1200#, and 2000# metallographic sandpapers, successively. These sheets were weighed first, then washed by acetone and ethanol to remove surface contaminants. 1 M HCl was chosen as the corrosion solution. Various concentrations of CeCDs or IL-CeCDs are added when studying their effects. The immersion tests last 24 h. After the immersion test is completed, the sample surface will be rinsed with deionized water to remove corrosion products and residual solution. Then, the sample was soaked in an acid cleaning solution for 5 min to remove the rust layer. Ultrasonic treatment of the sample surface was performed in anhydrous ethanol for 5 min. Finally, accurately weigh the sample after drying its surface. The corrosion rate (mm/a) could be obtained by [15]:
(1)
r
corr
=
8.76 ×
10
4
（ m −
m
t
）
S
1
× t × ρ
where r
corr is uniform corrosion rate, mm/a; m is mass of steel sample before testing, g; m
t is mass of steel sample after testing, g; S
1 is total area of sample; ρ is density of sample, g/cm2; and t is time, h.

The structures of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs were characterized by Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, NICOLET 6700) and Ultraviolet-visible adsorption spectroscopy (UV–vis, Lambda 950). The bonding status and chemical composition of CDs and IL-CDs were characterized by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, AMICUS). The morphologies of CDs and IL-CDs were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, TF20). After weight loss tests, the morphologies of the corrosion inhibitor films were observed by Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, HETACHIS-4800) and Laser Confocal Microscope (LCM, Keinz VHX-7000N), and the components of adsorption films were analyzed by XPS.

The electrochemical measurements were conducted at room temperature, utilizing a Gamry Reference 600 electrochemical workstation equipped with a three-electrode cell system. The scanning potential range was set at 300 mv via open circuit potential (OCP), and the scanning rate was 0.1 mV/s. The duration of OCP scanning was 1800 s. During EIS testing, the scanning frequency ranged from 0.01 Hz to 100,000 Hz. Tafel curves were acquired by scanning the potential within a 300 mV range around the OCP with a scanning rate of 0.2 mV/s. The N80 steel sample, with a 1.0 cm2 surface area exposed to the solution, served as the working electrode. The platinum plate acted as the counter electrode. The saturated calomel served as the reference electrode.

Four representative models, including two distinct carbon dot structures (CeCD3N and CeCD4N) and their ionic liquid-modified counterparts (IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N), were formulated and studied. The molecular structures of these models were constructed and chemically optimized using the Materials Studio software. In Dmol3 calculations, the GGA-PBE was selected as the functional, the DNP was selected as the base group (equals to 4.4), the thermal tailing effect value was set as 0.005 Ha, the spins of all atoms are not restricted, and the convergence standard for energy is 1.0 × 10−5 Ha. There were many extra-nuclear electrons in Ce atom, so density functional semi-nuclear pseudopotential (DSPP) was used to calculate Ce atom. Considering its relativistic effect, the remaining N, C, H atoms were calculated with all electrons. The frontier orbital distribution, energy, global activity, local activity, and electrostatic potential distribution of all models were calculated.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were performed to determine the statistical adsorption properties of corrosion inhibitors on metal surfaces. All simulations were based on the Forcite module in Materials Studio. The adsorption models of corrosion inhibitor molecules on iron surface in 1 M HCl solution were constructed in Amorphous Cell module. The Fe(110) surface model was constructed, with the surface size of 39.58 Å × 39.58 Å and the thickness of 10 atom layers. The solution phase contains 1964 water molecules, 36 hydronium ions, 36 chloride ions, and 1 (or 4) CDs molecules. The models were first optimized using the Smart algorithm. Then, a dynamical equilibrium simulation process was performed. The NVT ensemble was selected and the temperature was set as 298 K. The COMPASSII force field was selected to describe all the inter- and intramolecular interactions. The Ewald and Atom based summation methods were used to describe the electrostatic and vdW interactions, respectively. The cutoff distance was set as 12.5 Å. The time step was set as 1.0 fs, and the total simulation duration lasted 2.0 ns. During MD simulations, the model snapshots will be saved every 500 time steps. Finally, the last 100 frames (400 ps) were selected for the analyses of structural, dynamical, and energetic properties.

The TEM images of the synthesized CeCDs and IL-CeCDs are depicted in Fig. 1(a) and 1(b), respectively. The size of CeCDs ranges from 1.5 to 4.0 nm, whereas the size range of IL-CeCDs is from 2.0 to 6.0 nm. The modification of ionic liquids results in a larger size of CDs. In addition, it is observed that CeCDs exhibit partial agglomeration, whereas IL-CDs display a uniform dispersion, suggesting that IL-CDs possess better dispersibility compared to CDs.

From the infrared spectrum in Fig. 1(c), the peaks at 1284.6, 1471.7 cm−1 are caused by sp hybrid carbon atoms (C-N and C-O). The peaks at 1564.3, 1639.4, 1698.3 cm−1 are attributed to the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, specifically manifested through CN, -CO-NH-, and CO. The absorption peaks at 2742.8, and 3418.7 cm−1 correspond to C-H, and N-H bonds, respectively [16]. According to Fig. 1(d), the UV absorption spectrum of the CeCDs exhibits two distinct peaks, specifically located at 238 and 338 nm. The first peak arises from π-π* electron transition in the CC bond, while the second peak originates from n-π* electron transition between CN and C-O [17]. By modifying the ionic liquid, CeCDs are endowed with diversified functional groups, which results in significant differences between IL-CeCDs and CeCDs in their infrared spectra. At the same time, this modification method successfully preserves the original structural integrity of the carbon dots, thereby explaining the consistency in their ultraviolet spectral peaks.

The obtained CeCDs and IL-CeCDs exhibit similar elemental compositions but vary in the contents (Table S1). XPS analyses further confirm that CeCDs and IL-CeCDs have similar binding structures on the surface of N80 (Fig. 1(e) to 1(i)). From the deconvoluted C 1 s peaks, the peaks at 284.4, 286.8, and 288.3 eV correspond to C-C/CC, C-O/CN, and CO bonds, respectively [18]. The N 1 s spectrum of CeCDs exhibits three distinct peak features. Specifically, the peak at 398.2 eV is primarily attributed to the presence of pyridinic nitrogen, and this peak may also contain contributions from Ce-N bonds, suggesting that cerium atoms may have successfully coordinated with pyridinic nitrogen atoms within the carbon dots. The peak at 399.5 eV is mainly assigned to pyrrolic nitrogen, which typically exists in five-membered ring structures and is connected to surrounding carbon atoms through C-N bonds. Finally, the peak at 401.1 eV represents the presence of graphitic nitrogen, which usually embeds into the structure of graphite layers in place of carbon atoms, forming C-N bonds similar to those in graphite [19,43]. Notably, the modification of imidazolium ionic liquid leads to an enhanced content of pyrrole-like N, which could potentially bolster the adsorption strength of carbon dots and consequently improve their corrosion inhibition performance. In the Ce 3d spectrum, the two distinct peaks can be attributed to the presence of Ce3+ and Ce4+ ions, respectively [20]. However, in the IL-CeCDs, due to the modifying effect of the ionic liquid, the content of Ce elements is significantly reduced. The findings regarding chemical composition and bonding status are in alignment with FTIR and UV–vis analyses.

The corrosion behavior of N80 steel in 1 M HCl solution was investigated by the weight loss method. To determine the optimal doping concentration of Ce(NO3)3 for Ce doping on CDs, five values of 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μg/L of Ce(NO3)3 were used during the preparation of CeCDs. Fig. 2(a) presents the corrosion rates and inhibition efficiencies of CeCDs with various doping concentrations and a fixed dosing concentration of 200 mg/L. It can be seen that as the Ce doping concentration increases, the corrosion inhibition efficiency initially rises and then declines. Specifically, from 100 to 200 μg/L, the corrosion inhibition efficiency experiences a substantial enhancement, achieving over 80 %. However, with a gradual increase of doping amount up to 500 μg/L, the corrosion inhibition efficiency begins to decrease, peaking at 200 μg/L. Therefore, the 200 μg/L Ce doping amount is selected to prepare CeCDs for the following studies.

From Fig. 2(b), as the concentration of CeCDs increases, the corrosion inhibition effect gradually improves and eventually reaches 80 %. Once the concentration exceeds 200 mg/L, this upward trend stops. As the concentration rises to 300 and 400 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition effect fluctuates to 81.56 % and 79.88 %, respectively. High concentrations of CeCDs (cerium-doped carbon dots) can intensify the intermolecular π-π interactions, subsequently promoting agglomeration of the carbon dots and reducing the number of exposed effective active adsorption sites. This agglomeration phenomenon results in a less dense structure of the carbon dot film, which in turn weakens its adsorption capacity, ultimately manifesting as decreased corrosion inhibition performance. Fig. 3(c) depicts the effect diagram of the IL-CeCDs inhibitor. It can be seen that the rising concentration of IL-CeCDs increases its corrosion inhibition efficiency or decreases the corrosion rate of N80 carbon steel. At 50 mg/L, the corrosion rate is only 1.52 mm/a, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency is as high as 91.32 %. When the concentration of IL-CeCDs is increased to 100, 150, and 200 mg/L, the corrosion rates reach 1.36, 0.81, and 1.07 mm/a, respectively. The corrosion inhibition efficiency fluctuates slightly, but the largest value reaches 95.35 % at 150 mg/L. The corrosion inhibition performance of IL-CeCDs is obviously better than that of CeCDs. When the concentration exceeds 100 mg/L, the addition of ionic liquid significantly enhances the corrosion inhibition performance. This is because the grafting of ionic liquids enhances the dispersion stability of CeCDs in aqueous solution. There are two potential reasons for this phenomenon: Firstly, the amino-terminated ionic liquid facilitates the ionization of carbon dots in the aqueous solution; Secondly, the amino-terminated ionic liquid positively charges the carbon dots. The two reasons cause them to repel each other, thereby improving their water solubility.

By calculating the adsorption isotherm, the interaction strength between the corrosion inhibitor and the metal surface can be evaluated to determine the corrosion inhibition mechanism. By calculating the adsorption free energy (
∆
G
ads
0
) [21], the adsorption type can be identified. The calculation method is as follows [22]:
θ
1 − θ
=
K
ads
C
∆
G
ads
0
= − RT ln （ 1000
K
ads
）
where, θ, K
ads , and C represent the efficiency, equilibrium constant, and concentration of corrosion inhibitor, respectively. The adsorption isotherm of corrosion inhibitor can be obtained with C as abscissa and
C
θ
as ordinate. R and T represent molar gas constant and absolute temperature (298 K), respectively. When the
∆
G
ads
0
exceeds −20 kJ/mol, the corrosion inhibitor undergoes physical adsorption. When the
∆
G
ads
0
falls below −40 kJ/mol, the corrosion inhibitor undergoes chemical adsorption [23,24]. From Fig. 3(d), the
∆
G
ads
0
values of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs are determined to be −25.87 and −32.02 kJ/mol, respectively, indicating that both inhibitors exhibit physical and chemical mixed adsorption. The R
2 values of both inhibitors are very close to 1.0, indicating that they conform to the Langmuir adsorption model. The K
ads values of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs stand at 34.15 and 407.19 g/L, respectively. The larger K
ads value represents a significant improvement in adsorption capacity due to the modification of ionic liquids, which on one hand reduces the agglomeration of carbon dots, and on the other hand provides stronger adsorption sites.

Fig. 3(a) to 3(e) exhibit the electrochemical Nyquist plots of CeCDs with different Ce doping concentrations (100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μg/L) at different dosing concentrations (0, 50, 100, and 150 mg/L). The fitting parameters are shown in Table 1. To more accurately model and fit the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data, we employed the equivalent circuit diagram shown in Fig. 3(f). In this circuit, R
s represents the solution resistance, while R
ct denotes the charge transfer resistance. Furthermore, the CPE (Constant Phase Element) is used to represent the double-layer characteristics, where the size of the CPE is determined by relevant parameters, and n reflects the phase shift caused by surface inhomogeneity [41]. For the blank sample (dosing concentration equals to 0), the impedance arc radius is small reflecting severe corrosion. After the addition of CeCDs, the impedance arc radius and impedance value of the N80 steel sheet undergoes significant enhancement. Specifically, when the doping concentration of Ce is 100 μg/L and the dosing concentration of CeCDs is 150 mg/L, the impedance value attains its maximum, achieving 674.1 Ω/cm2. However, upon further addition of CeCDs, the impedance value does not increase but rather decreases, suggesting that the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the CeCDs corrosion inhibitor has already peaked. When the doping concentration of Ce is 200 μg/L and the dosing concentration of CeCDs is 150 mg/L, the impedance value attains its maximum, reaching 877 Ω/cm2. Similarly, for Ce doping concentrations of 300, 400, and 500 μg/L, the respective maximum R
ct values are 824.3, 715.4, and 772.4 Ω/cm2, with the optimal CeCDs concentration consistently being 150 mg/L. Therefore, within the doping concentration range of 100–500 μg/L, the optimal CeCDs concentration remains at 150 mg/L. Notably, when the Ce doping concentration reaches 200 μg/L, the optimal concentration of 150 mg/L exhibits the most effective corrosion inhibition, achieving a corrosion inhibition rate of 95.44 %. The OCP test results for CeCDs and IL-CeCDs are shown in Fig. S3. In solutions containing CeCDs and IL-CeCDs, the OCP of carbon steel is significantly elevated compared to solutions without inhibitors. This outcome indicates that both CeCDs and IL-CeCDs can provide effective corrosion protection for carbon steel. Furthermore, as the concentration of these two inhibitors increases, the OCP also rises accordingly, indicating a consistent trend with the EIS results.

Further, the potentiodynamic polarization curves of above systems were also determined ( Fig. 4). By the classical extrapolation method, the relevant parameters of the corrosion potential (E
corr ), corrosion current density (i
corr ), and Tafel slope (β
a , β
c ) were obtained and exhibited in Table 2. The corrosion inhibition efficiency was calculated as follows [25,26]:
η =
i
corr
0
−
i
corr
i
corr
0
where,
i
corr
0
and
i
corr
are the self-corrosion current densities in the blank control group and the experimental group, respectively. Based on the data analysis presented in Table 2, it is evident that both CeCDs and IL-CeCDs exhibit displacement in E
corr below −85 mV, classifying them as mixed-type inhibitors [42,44]. In the untreated blank solution, the i
corr reaches a significant level of 514.49 μA·cm⁻², substantially exceeding the values observed in the presence of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs at various concentrations, thereby indicating excellent corrosion inhibition effects. As the concentrations of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs increase, they both demonstrate a decreasing trend in i
corr . Notably, at IL-CeCDs concentrations of 50 and 150 mg/L, the corrosion current density drops to 47.853 and 11.332 μA·cm⁻², respectively, corresponding to inhibition efficiencies of 90.70 % and 97.80 %. These data not only underscore the excellent corrosion inhibition performance of IL-CeCDs across different concentrations, but also emphasize its exceptional efficiency at higher concentration (150 mg/L).

The SEM morphologies of N80 steel exhibits varying degrees of surface corrosion without or with the addition of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs to 1 M HCl solutions ( Fig. 5(a) to 5(d)). Before the addition of inhibitors, the steel surface exhibits severe corrosion. However, upon the addition of CeCDs, the surface becomes moderately corroded. Remarkably, the corrosion becomes nearly undetectable after the inclusion of IL-CeCDs. This observation unequivocally demonstrates the superior corrosion inhibition performance of IL-CeCDs. Likewise, the LCS images in Fig. 5(e) to 5(i) also substantiate the aforementioned disparities in corrosion morphology. The steel surface displays only fundamental mechanical scratches, with a maximum corrosion height (S
z ) of 5.41 μm and an arithmetic mean height (S
a ) of 0.43 μm. However, after immersion in HCl solution for 24 h, numerous bumps emerge, resulting in an increased roughness with a S
z value of 18.7 μm and a S
a value of 1.12 μm. It is noteworthy that the addition of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs leads to the reduction of S
z to 8.52 μm and 4.25 μm, and S
a to 0.30 μm and 0.25 μm, respectively. These observations clearly exhibit the corrosion inhibition effect of the prepared IL-CeCDs.

XPS analysis reveals that upon exposure of N80 steel to CeCDs and IL-CeCDs solutions, a new element N emerges on the steel surface (Fig. 5(j) to 5(m)). This confirms that both corrosion inhibitors effectively bond with the steel’s surface layer. The N 1 s spectrum exhibits three significant peaks. The first peak corresponds to the N atom (CN) connected to C of CeCDs. The second peak at 398.8 eV indicates that the CeCDs inhibitor captures N atoms (N-Fe) interacting with the steel surface. The third peak at 401.4 eV suggests that the CeCDs inhibitor captures nitrogen protons (N-H) [27,28]. These data lead to the conclusion that the presence of N signifies the successful adsorption of the corrosion inhibitor on the steel surface. In the Ce 3d spectrum, the four fitted peaks are assigned to CeO2, Ce2O3, CeO2, and CeN, respectively [29]. This indicates that the steel surface is covered with a corrosion inhibitor that contains both passivation and adsorption films, thereby delaying the corrosion process. The passivation film is mainly composed of cerium-containing oxides and hydroxides, while the adsorption film is primarily made up of freshly prepared carbon dots. Moreover, the adsorption mode of the corrosion inhibitor encompasses both chemical and physical adsorption, aligning with the findings of adsorption isotherm research.

After the application of varying concentrations of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs, the SEM images of the metal surface are carefully scrutinized. The blank sample ( Fig. 6(i)) exhibits a profoundly intricate concave state, cloaking a thick layer of corrosion residue. Upon introducing 50 mg/L of CeCDs (Fig. 6(a)), the surface displays a relatively smooth texture, with a significant reduction in corrosion products. This trend becomes increasingly apparent as the concentration of CeCDs escalates. At 150 mg/L (Fig. 6(c)), the surface cracks on the steel sheet are notably diminished, and the corrosion inhibitor film appears denser, indicating that a higher concentration of CeCDs fosters the development of a thicker adsorption film. Further elemental analysis of the surface in Fig. 6(j) reveals the presence of the Ce element, confirming that the corrosion inhibitor has adhered to the metal surface. This finding underscores the positive influence of the Ce element in enhancing the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the steel surface. Compared with CeCDs, the effect of IL-CeCDs (Fig. 6(e∼h, k)) is greatly enhanced. Across all concentrations, the surface remains virtually devoid of corrosion traces, and the corrosion inhibitor films adhere uniformly to the steel surface, leaving almost no corrosion products behind. Notably, the original mechanical scratches on the steel are still clearly visible, suggesting minimal corrosion. In contrast to CeCDs without grafted ionic liquids, the corrosion inhibitor film formed by IL-CeCDs is significantly denser and more uniform, with no signs of damage or cracking. The film reaches its peak density at 150 mg/L. These observations align with the conclusions drawn from electrochemical and weight-loss methods for evaluating corrosion inhibitors.

Fig. S2 compares the zeta potentials of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs. Results indicate that CeCDs exhibit a negative potential range from −10 to −18 mV, suggesting insufficient electrostatic repulsion between molecules (i.e., tend to aggregate) in solution. This can affect the corrosion inhibition performance of CeCDs. In contrast, IL-CeCDs display a positive potential of approximately 44–49 mV, significantly enhancing its dispersion in solution and reducing agglomeration. Therefore, from the perspective of zeta potential, IL-CeCDs demonstrate superior dispersion compared to CeCDs, facilitating the formation of a more uniform and denser corrosion inhibitor film.

Based on XPS analysis, which revealed the valence states of Ce to be + 3 and + 4, two distinct carbon dot structures (CeCD3N and CeCD4N) were devised. Furthermore, their ionic liquid-modified counterparts (IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N) were also formulated. To gain insights into the impact of Ce doping and ionic liquid modification on their properties, molecular simulation studies were conducted.

Fig. 7(a) provides the HOMO and LUMO orbital distributions of four molecules. As observed, all four molecules exhibit cyclic structures, with the HOMO and LUMO orbits concentrated on these cyclic structures. This is attributed to their planar configurations, which facilitate the formation of conjugated large π bonds. These conjugated bonds enhance the reactivity of the molecules. To investigate the overall reactivity of these molecules, additional calculations were performed to determine the parameters such as the energy gap (ΔE), chemical potential (μ), absolute hardness (η), global softness (S), and electrophilic index (ω) [30–32]. These parameters characterize the comprehensive reactivity of the molecules and are presented in Table S2. Ionic liquid modification significantly enhances the HOMO and LUMO orbital energies of carbon dots, while moderately reducing their energy gap value [33]. Consequently, IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N exhibit lower μ values. Furthermore, CeCD3N and IL-CeCD3N possess narrower energy gaps, lower η values, higher ω and S values, theoretically indicating their superior reactivity.

Fig. 7(b) depicts the differential charge density distributions [34] of the four molecules. It is evident that the Ce atom loses electrons, and the majority of these electrons are concentrated in the ring center. Notably, the Ce atoms in CeCD3N and IL-CeCD3N lose more electrons, potentially accounting for their heightened activity. Fig. 7(c) illustrates the electrostatic potential distributions of the four molecules, revealing that the Ce atoms are positively charged due to their electron lose. Compared with CeCD3N and CeCD4N, the Ce atoms in IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N exhibit a stronger positive charge, though the planar positive charge is weaker. Additionally, the ionic liquids also display a stronger positive charge. Both ionic liquids and Ce atoms serve as potent active centers.

The Fukui index [35] and Mulliken charge distribution for each atom in the molecules have been calculated and are presented in Table S3. In CeCD3N, the atoms Ce23, C59, H73, and O77 exhibit significant nucleophilic and electrophilic Fukui indices, indicating their strong ability to gain and lose electrons. In CeCD4N, Ce78, C57, H71, H56, H63, and O72 possess large nucleophilic Fukui indices, implying their strong electron-gaining capacity, while Ce78, C57, H60, and O75 have substantial electrophilic Fukui indices, indicating their strong electron-losing ability. In IL-CeCD3N, Ce23, C85, and C123 possess significant nucleophilic and electrophilic Fukui indices, suggesting their strong ability to gain and lose electrons. In IL-CeCD4N, Ce22, C84, and C122 possess large nucleophilic Fukui index, implying their strong electron acquisition ability. However, Ce22, C84 and O74 in IL-CeCD4N, they have both large nucleophilic and electrophilic Fukui indices, indicating their robust capacity for both electron gain and loss.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were conducted to investigate the interfacial adsorption properties of four corrosion inhibitor models on the metal surface in HCl solution. Ce will be adsorbed on the metal surface from the carbon dots, and then the carbon dots and Ce will be adsorbed on the metal surface together, as shown in Fig. S1. This shows that the carbon dots and the oxide of Ce play a protective role together, which verifies the XPS analysis. As depicted in Fig. 8(a), the mean square displacement (MSD) [36] curves indicate that the diffusion coefficient (D) [37] of the solution containing IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N is significantly lower than that of the solution with CeCD3N and CeCD4N. This observation indicates that IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N are more effective at restricting the movement of the corrosive solution, thereby enhancing metal protection. In addition, as shown in Fig. 8(b), the number of solution molecules adsorbed on the metal surface decreases after the addition of IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N. This is attributed to the wider coverage area of these two molecules, which can effectively repel solution molecules, leading to an improved protective effect. Fig. 8(c) depicts the relative concentration distribution of four kinds of corrosion inhibitors. It can be found that IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N exhibit a higher concentration near the metal surface, whereas CeCD3N and CeCD4N tend to distribute more distantly from the metal surface. This indicates that IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N are capable of forming a denser corrosion inhibitor film, while the more distant distribution of CeCD3N and CeCD4N may result in weaker adsorption strength [38–40]. Fig. 8(d) illustrates the average interaction energy between the four types of molecules and the metal surface, considering both a single molecule and four molecules. When a single corrosion inhibitor molecule is present, the adsorption energy of CeCD3N and CeCD4N is observed to be slightly lower than that of IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N. This disparity is attributed to the larger adsorption area of IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N. However, when the number of molecules increases to four, a noteworthy trend emerges: the adsorption energies of CeCD3N and CeCD4N drop greatly, whereas the adsorption energies of IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N experience a slight increase. This phenomenon, when coupled with the insights from Fig. 8(c), suggests that the intermolecular bonding between CeCD3N and CeCD4N molecules is not as robust, making it challenging for them to form a dense molecular film. Conversely, the molecules of IL-CeCD3N and IL-CeCD4N tend to interconnect, resulting in a stronger adsorption strength.

The adsorption patterns of CeCDs and IL-CeCDs are summarized and presented in Fig. 9. During the adsorption process of CeCDs, Ce atoms can detach from the carbon dots and adhere to the metal surface. Once separated from the carbon dots, Ce allows the carbon dots to adsorb onto the metal surface in a parallel arrangement, aligning alongside Ce to jointly construct a protective barrier for corrosive particles. The Ce and N co-doped carbon dots, leveraging both physical and chemical adsorption mechanisms, forming a protective film on the metal surface, effectively blocking the direct contact between corrosive ions and the metal. This synergistic effect not only enhances the binding strength between the corrosion inhibitor and the metal interface, but also significantly improves the stability and durability of the corrosion inhibitor, as depicted in Fig. 9(a). However, when the concentration of CeCDs becomes excessively high, they tend to cluster together, hindering the formation of a dense protective film, as illustrated in Fig. 9(b). Conversely, after modification with ionic liquids, the increased molecular weight of IL-CeCDs enables them to intertwine with each other and adsorb with Ce atoms to form a more compact protective film, thus protecting the metal more effectively, as shown in Fig. 9(c).

Our findings highlight the vast potential of carbon dot corrosion inhibitors in the field of metal protection. Future development trends include doping and functional modification to enhance the performance in more complex and harsh environments, optimizing synthesis processes to reduce energy consumption, composite applications with other additive materials to enhance performance, and deepening the understanding of corrosion inhibition mechanisms. These research efforts will propel the more efficient and environmentally friendly application of carbon dot corrosion inhibitors in industrial fields.

In summary, CeCDs were successfully synthesized and further grafted with amino-terminated imidazolium ionic liquids for IL-CeCDs to boost performance. Optimal cerium doping was 200 μg/L, yielding a peak corrosion inhibition rate of 80.82 %. IL grafting significantly raised inhibition to over 92 % from ∼80 %, due to improved CeCDs dispersion, aiding dense film formation. IL-CeCDs exhibited 91.32 %-95.35 % inhibition at 50–200 mg/L, peaking at 150 mg/L, then plateauing. Adherence followed Langmuir isotherms, suggesting chemical adsorption dominance with physical contribution. Surface analysis showed a hybrid film of CeCDs and cerium passivation on metal. Molecular simulations illuminated the mechanism: cerium as a key reactor, IL modification enhancing CeCDs activity, reducing agglomeration, and promoting denser films.

Songqing Hu: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Shuangqing Sun: Supervision, Software, Methodology. Yu Fu: Software, Methodology. Miantuo Li: Formal analysis, Data curation. Nannan Cui: Software, Methodology. Wentao Qu: Methodology, Investigation. Huiling Su: Methodology, Investigation. Yi Zhou: Investigation, Data curation. Zhikun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Fengting Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52204066, 52474020), the Shandong Province Youth Entrepreneurship Technology Support Program for Higher Education Institutions (2023KJ060), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (23CX06018A).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.136132.
